answer:
**In the Old English poem *Deor* (chapter 13 of *Old English Elegies*), there are seven strophes (or stanzas), each corresponding to one of the seven paragraphs in the translation.[^1]**

The refrain—"That passed over; so may this." (rendering Old English *Þæs ofereode, þisses swā mæg*)—appears at the end of the first **five strophes** (¶¶ 1–5, on Weland, Beadohild, Mæðhild, Theodric, and Eormanric).[^2][^3][^4][^5][^6]

It **ceases** in the sixth strophe (a gnomic reflection on sorrow and divine fortune).[^7]

The refrain then **returns** once more in the seventh and final strophe (Deor's personal lament about losing his position to Heorrenda).[^8]

Thus, there are **five strophes** before the refrain ceases.[^1]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch.13 *Deor*, [chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [¶1](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0).

[^3]: Ibid., [¶2](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-d11ebc).

[^4]: Ibid., [¶3](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-5821a4).

[^5]: Ibid., [¶4](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-ac23ab).

[^6]: Ibid., [¶5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-160f1c).

[^7]: Ibid., [¶6](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-b4d6b7).

[^8]: Ibid., [¶7](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).
